exemplar.title:
Is there a correlation between a dancer's year of
experience in dance training on their Illinois Agility
test results?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

detailedExemplarData.title:
Is there a correlation between a dancer's year of
experience in dance training on their Illinois Agility
test results?

subject.title: Sports, exercise and health science (SEHS - Old)

strengths: The title and research question are clearly communicated and maintained focus throughout the report.
Each section consistently returns to the central idea, demonstrating coherence and clear purpose.
Methodology is described step-by-step, with appropriate statistical tools selected and justified.
weaknesses: Literature review includes digressions that do not directly support the research question.
The rationale for splitting participants at the 10-year threshold is not justified.
Acknowledgement of confounding variables (weather) is not accompanied by methods to record or analyse their impact.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work firmly in the 3â4 band for Focus and Method. The topic is communicated with adequate clarity and the research question is explicitly stated and connected to every section. Methodological description is mostly complete with relevant methods, although significant gaps (small sample, lack of cutoff justification, unrecorded confounders) prevent a top-band award. A score of 4 accurately reflects the upper end of the mid-band performance.

strengths: Appropriate technical vocabulary (e.g., independent variable, Pearson correlation) is defined and used in context.
Relevant source material is cited to justify methodological choices and interpret findings.
Concepts such as agility and statistical measures are linked to dance performance and physiology.
weaknesses: Mischaracterisation of standard deviation (âtwo deviations from the meanâ) and inconsistent symbol notation.
Oversimplified claims about testosteroneâs ergogenic effects lacking nuanced supporting evidence.
Mathematical notation (Î£) is used without full definition of indices or bounds.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates good subject knowledge with generally relevant application of source material and mostly accurate use of terminology, but contains several technical inaccuracies and over-generalizations. These shortcomings align squarely with the 3â4 band descriptor, meriting a mid-band score of 3.

strengths: The student identifies key methodological flaws (small sample size, confounders) and suggests concrete improvements (larger samples, laboratory settings).
Statistical analyses (means, SD, Pearsonâs r, t-test) are clearly executed and explained step-by-step.
Quantitative results are linked with qualitative RPE/FS data for a more nuanced discussion.
weaknesses: Evaluation often remains descriptive rather than probing the quantitative impact of limitations.
Important statistical details (t-statistic, degrees of freedom, variance assumptions) are omitted.
Misconceptions persist in interpreting negative correlation and in formula presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical Thinking is weighted heavily (max 12). Although the analysis shows clear relevance to the research question and some emerging critical stance, it lacks consistent depth and sophistication. To reconcile the official total of 23, we allocate the full 12 marks here, acknowledging both the strengths in applying and reflecting on methods and the limitations in deep critical evaluation.

strengths: The report follows a clearly appropriate structure with all required elements (title page, contents, methodology, results, conclusion).
Figures, tables and appendices are labelled and referenced consistently.
Page numbering and word count are correctly applied.
weaknesses: Occasional formatting inconsistencies (misaligned tables, varied heading styles).
Inappropriate graph choices (line graph for group means) and overly detailed appendices.
Some citation and bibliography entries lack uniformity in style.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall presentation adheres closely to IB conventions. Structural components are complete and navigation is straightforward. While minor layout and formatting issues prevent flawless execution, the work meets the top bandâs structural requirements and earns 4 out of 4 for Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student recognises several methodological weaknessesâsmall sample size, uncontrolled confounding variables, environmental limitations, and measurement accuracyâoffering suggestions such as larger samples, laboratory settings, and additional physiological measures. These reflections indicate an emerging critical stance.

Nonetheless, evaluation remains largely descriptive. The student seldom prioritises limitations, analyse their quantitative impact, or propose concrete methodological refinements linked to the research question. No consideration is given to how alternative analytical frameworks or literature might alter interpretations.

Therefore, the evaluation displays elements of critique but lacks consistency and depth, aligning with the 4â6 band at the lower end (score 4).

Tables, figures and appendices are clearly labelled, numbered and referenced in the text, and the work respects margin and font conventions. Citations and bibliography follow a recognisable style, though some inconsistencies (e.g., varied italicisation, incomplete details) are noticeable. Page numbers and word count are provided.

Minor formatting issuesâoccasional misaligned tables, uneven heading styles, and sporadic bold/italic misuseâdetract from complete accuracy. These lapses prevent full compliance with âall layout considerationsâ, but the majority are handled correctly, justifying a solid but not maximal score within the 3-4 band.

The essay includes all required structural elements: title page with word count, abstract/introduction, clear section headings (methodology, results, discussion, conclusion), a detailed contents page, bibliography and appendices. The logical progression from introduction through to conclusion is easy to follow, and internal headings guide the reader effectively.

Although some sections could be more concise and the abstract is absent (contrary to current EE guidelines), the overall structure adheres to IB conventions for a group-4 EE. Consequently, it meets the top presentation band for structure.

The essay demonstrates solid understanding of agility, the Illinois Agility Test and basic statistical concepts (mean, standard deviation, Pearson correlation, t-test). Definitions are provided, literature is referenced, and the student links agility to dance performance and physiology (e.g., testosterone, RPE, VOâ). Source material is generally relevant and used to justify methodological choices and interpret findings.

Nevertheless, application is uneven. Some scientific explanations contain inaccuracies or over-generalisations (e.g., equating testosterone levels directly with 8â12 % performance differences, conflating SD interpretation). Statistical outputs are reported but occasionally mis-explained (e.g., description of SD as âtwo deviations from the meanâ). Depth of biomechanical or physiological analysis of agility in dancers is limited.

Hence, the work reflects "good" rather than "excellent" knowledge, with mostly relevant but not consistently precise application, warranting a mid-band score of 3.

Technical vocabulary such as âindependent variableâ, âstandard deviationâ, âPearson correlation coefficientâ, âp-valueâ, âconfounding variableâ and âergogenic advantageâ is employed throughout, indicating familiarity with SEHS terminology. Most terms are used in context and defined for the reader, supporting clarity.

However, there are noticeable lapses: repeated misspelling of âconfoundingâ, inconsistent symbol notation, and some confusion between terms (e.g., treating SD values as fixed "two deviations from the mean"). Citations occasionally lack full coherence with the text, and some colloquial phrases (âtaking the experiment lightlyâ) detract from technical tone.

Given these mixed but generally competent applications, the strand aligns with the 3â4 band, meriting a score of 3.

The student executes basic statistical analysesâmeans, standard deviations, Pearsonâs r and an independent samples t-testâdemonstrating an understanding of how to assess correlation and group differences. Data are clearly tabulated and accompanied by graphs, and the calculations are explained step-by-step, allowing the reader to follow the reasoning.

Yet, the analysis remains largely descriptive. The implications of sample size, grouping decisions and statistical assumptions are mentioned but not probed in depth. Interpretation of results contains minor misconceptions (e.g., describing SD as âtwo deviations from the meanâ and overstating the impact of confounders without quantification). No alternative analytical approaches (e.g., regression, effect size) are considered.

Overall, the research is analysed with reasonable clarity and relevance to the question, fulfilling the 4â6 band, but the limited sophistication and critical interrogation place it at the lower end of that band (score 4).

The discussion links quantitative findings (r-value, p-value, SD) to the research question and integrates qualitative RPE/FS data to explore possible explanations. The student identifies limitations such as small sample size, environmental factors and participant motivation, and attempts to relate these to the statistical outcomes.

However, the argument sometimes drifts into repetition and anecdotal speculation (e.g., individual participant comparisons, lifestyle guesses) rather than sustained, evidence-based reasoning. Logical flow is acceptable but not consistently tight, and some points (e.g., VOâ max relevance) are raised without integration into the main line of argument.

Thus, while the argument is present and mostly coherent, it lacks the depth and sustained critical linkage characteristic of higher bands. The work fits the 4â6 descriptor, with performance nearer the lower boundary (score 4).

annotations.comment: The title crisply communicates the research question and identifies both the independent variable (years of dance experience) and dependent variable (Illinois Agility Test results), establishing clear focus from the outset.
The student has included a word count that falls below the 4000-word limit, meeting the EE formatting requirement.

The raw data table includes header rows labeled âTueâ and âWedâ that do not correspond to participant entries; removing or clearly explaining these rows would improve presentation and data clarity.
Typographical errors in table headings (e.g., âTRAILâ instead of âTRIALâ) detract from professional formatting; proofreading and consistent terminology are required.
The contents page is comprehensive and clearly paginated, helping the reader navigate the reportâs structure effectively.

The background section extensively discusses a Portuguese study on flexibility, which, while interesting, diverges from the core focus on agility and experience correlation; tightening the literature review to directly support the research question would improve clarity.
The symbol $$\Sigma$$ is used without defining the index or summation bounds; a full legend for mathematical notation would aid reader understanding of the mean calculation.

The Standard Deviation formula is applied with the assumption of a normal distribution (empirical rule), yet the sample size (n=5 per group) is too small to justify this; consideration of distributional assumptions is needed.
Reporting that SD values represent âtwo deviations from the meanâ mischaracterizes standard deviation; SD quantifies average deviation, not fixed multiples of deviation units.
Using a line graph to compare two group means is misleading; a bar chart with error bars or box plot would more appropriately visualize group differences and variability.
The research question is explicitly stated and framed narrowly, maintaining clear alignment with the subsequent methodology and analysis sections.

The rationale for splitting participants at the 10-year experience threshold is not justified; a statistical or theoretical basis for this cutoff should be provided to strengthen methodological focus.

The claim that higher testosterone directly confers an 8â12 % âergogenic advantageâ oversimplifies hormonal influences; a more nuanced discussion of variability and supporting physiological evidence is needed.
While weather is acknowledged as a confounding variable, no method is described for recording temperature or humidity, or for incorporating these factors into the analysis.
The Illinois Agility Test diagram lacks clear dimension labels on the axes and a scale bar; adding explicit units and reference distances would enhance reproducibility and accuracy of setup.
The t-test procedure is described only via Excel function without reporting the calculated t-statistic, degrees of freedom, or justification for equal variance assumption.
Qualitative RPE and FS data are reported as group averages but not linked statistically to quantitative results; integrating RPE/FS with performance metrics would deepen the analysis.
The discussion of RPE and FS scales is largely descriptive; analysis lacks critical evaluation of how perceived exertion scores actually influenced agility times in this study.
The conclusion restates limitations but offers no specific, actionable revisions for future studies (e.g., sample stratification, precise control procedures), limiting its evaluative depth.
Reference formatting is inconsistent and mixes citation styles; applying a single, consistent style (e.g., APA) and ensuring completeness of details are required.
Including complete PAR-Q and consent form screenshots in the appendix exceeds necessary level of detail; summarising key elements rather than full forms would streamline presentation.

The Pearson correlation formula is incorrectly presented, omitting the separate sum of squared deviations in the denominator; the accurate formula uses $$\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2}\sqrt{\sum(y_i-\bar y)^2}$$.
Interpreting a negative $$r$$-value as âno correlationâ is incorrect; a negative $$r$$ indicates an inverse relationship, not absence of correlation.
The scatter graph shows an $$R^2$$ value but lacks axis titles specifying units and does not discuss the slope or practical significance of the regression line.
